FETn PoIITICaI PnaCTICES CoTT,IMISSIoN
428 J Street. Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
luly 17,2003
Danny Weil, Ph.D., J.D.
The Critical Thinking Institute
25I Campodonico Avenue
Guadalupe, CA93434
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-03-098
Dear Dr. Weil:
This letter is in response to your request for assistance on behalf of the City of
Guadalupe Mayor Pro Tem Joe Talaugon regarding the provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act').1 Because the facts y& t ur" preseited are not sufficient to render formal
advice, we are treating yourfequest as one for informal assistance (regulation
18329b)Q)@).)2 Our assistanie is based on the facts presented; the Eommission does
not act as a finder of fact when it provides informal assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 71.)
QUESTTONS
1. Does Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon have a conflict of interest in the city council,s
review of the grand jury report thatmay discuss the chamber of commerce when his
coffee shop is within 500 feet of the chamber?
2. Does Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon have a conflict of interest in the city council,s
discussion and vote regarding the downtown revitali zation since he owns acoffee shop
downtown?
I Govemment Code sections 81000 - gl}t4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not pro-vide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 14; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.j in addition, this leuer
should not be construed as assistance on any conduct that may have already taken place.


File No. I-03-098
Page No. 2
3. Does Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon have a conflict of interest in the city council's
discussion and vote regarding the use of redevelopment funds since he owns a coffee
shop downtown?
4. Would Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon be disqualified for 12 months if he sold his
coffee shop?
5. If Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon gives his coffee shop to an adult child, would he then
immediately be able to participate in decisions regarding the downtown area from which
he is currently disqualified because of the coffee shop?
CONCLUSIONS
l. Mayor Pro Tern Talaugon would have a conflict of interest in the city council's
review of the grand jury report that may discuss the chamber of commerce only if it is
reasonably foreseeable that his coffee shop will be materially financially effected as a
result of that discussion. We do not have enough facts to make this determination.
2. Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon would have a conflict of interest in the city council's
discussion and vote regarding the downtown revitalizationif it is reasonably foreseeable
that his coffee shop will be materially financially effected. I{, for instance, the downtown
revitalization project helps or hinders the downtown businesses, then a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect may be present for the mayor pro tem's coffee shop.
3. Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon would have a conflict of interest in the city council's
discussion and vote regarding the use of redevelopment funds if it is reasonably
' . foreseeable that his coffee shop will be materially financially effected. If for.instance,
the use of redevelopment funds helps or hinders the downtown businesses, then a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect may be present for the mayor pro tern's
coffee shop.
4. Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon would have an economic interest in the purchaser of his
coffee shop for 12 months after he sold his coffee shop.
5. By making a bona fide, irrevocable gift of his coffee shop to an adult child,
Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon still have an economic interest in the coffee shop for 12
months since the income he has received from the coffee shop is disquali$ingfor 12
months after reaching the $500 threshold for sources of income. Consequently, he will
have a conflict of interest in the decisions regarding the development of the downtown
area if there is a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the coffee shop since it wilt
remain an economic interest of his for 12 months.
FACTS
You represent Joe Talaugon, the mayorpro tem of the City of Guadalupe, a small
city of 5,800 people on the central coast of California. The mayor pro tem owns a small


File No. I-03-098
Page No. 3
business in downtown Guadalupe. It is a coffeehouse and ownership does not include the
building where the business is harbored. The revitalization of the downtown and
decisions about the use of redevelopment funds and related issues may affect businesses
or entities within 500 feet of his business.
During telephone conversations on May 23,2003 and June 17, 2003,you asked
an additional question on behalf of the mayor pro tem. You queried whether the mayor
pro tem could participate in the city council's discussions regarding a grand jury report
even though his coffee shop is located within 500 feet of the chamber of commerce,
which may be mentioned in the report. You also provided that the mayorpro tem has a
seven-year lease on the real property on which his business resides. This leases
colnmenced in 1998 and ends in 2005 and is renewable.
ANALYSIs
section 87100 prohibits any public official from "making," "participating in
making," or otherwise using his or her official position to "influence" a govemmental
decision in which the public official has a financial interest. The Commission has
adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an official has a
disqualifring conflict of interest (regulation 18700, subdivisions (b)(1) - (8), which is
discussed below. The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest may occur
whenever a public official makes a govemmental decision which mayhave a reasonably
foreseeable and material financial effect on one or more of his or her economic interests.
Steps 1. & 2. fs Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon a public official ttmaking.,,.oparticipating
in making." or 66influencing" a governmental decision?
i;.1. 
\ 
;,- ,,....-._. 
-.. :r.
The conflict-of-interest prohibition applies only to public officials. "Public
official" is defined as "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency. . . ." (Section 82048.) Since Mayor Pro Tern Talaugon is the mayor
pro tem of the City of Guadalupe, he is a public official who will be making, participating
in or influencing govemmental decisions within the meaning of the Act. (Section ST1OO;
regulations 787 01, 187 02.1 - 187 02.3.)
Step 3. What are Mayor Pro Tem Talaugonrs economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from
economic interests. The economic interests from which conflicts of interest may arise are
defined in regulations 18703-18703.5. Identifting which, if any, of these economic
interests are held by a public official is the third step in analyzing a potential conflict of
interest under the Act. (Regulation 18700(b)(3).) There are six possible economic
interests:
1. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation
I 8703.1(a));


File No. I-03-098
PageNo.4
2. A public official has an economic interest in which he or she is a director, fficer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d);
regulation I 8703. 1 (b));
3. A public official has an economic interest in real property inwhich he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation lS7O3.Z);
4. A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 I 03(c); regulation 187 03.3)3 ;
5. A public official has an economic interest in any source of Stfts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87 I 03(e); regulation 187 $.9;
6. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is known as
the "personal financial effects" rule (section 87103, regulation r s703.5).
The information provided only refers to Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon's coffee shop,
so this analysis will be limited to the economic interests that derive from it. Mayor pro
Tem Talaugon is the sole owner of his coffee shop.
The Coffee Shop: As sole owner of the coffee shop, the mayor pro tem has an
economic interest in this business entity as an officer/employee of the company as well as
an investor. (Sections 87103(a) and (d); regulation 18703.1(a) and O).) Additionally, he
has an economic interest in the shop because of the income he has received frorn it. , ., ,
(Section 87103(c).)
Customers: Pursuant to sections 87103(c) and 82030, Mayor Pro Tern Talaugon
would also have an interest in an individual who is a source of income to his shop sinie
his ownership interest in the coffee shop is 100%. He will have an economic interest in a
particular client, such as the chamber oico-m..ce, if the income attributed to him from
the client will aggregate to $500 or more during the relevant 12 month time period. Since
you have not provided facts relating to customers, we will forego any remaining analysis
of this economic interest." As to the chamber of commerce, if the association itself has
not been a client of his coffee shop, the chamber is not an economic interest of his.
Purchases of coffee from individual members are not aggregated. In other words, the
members'purchases do not count toward the $500. If the answer is no, then the chamber
is not an economic interest ofhis and the chamber's proximity to the coffee shop is
relevant to our conflicts analysis only if it is reasonably foreseeable that the coffee shop
itself is materially affected as discussed below under step four.
a You should request additional advice concerning the customers of the coffee shop if you believe
that they may be materially impacted by the decision.


File No. I-03-098
Page No. 5
ReaI Property: Under the Act, an "interest in real property" includes a leasehold
interest in real property within the official's jurisdiction. (Section82033.) The terms
'tnterest in real property" and "leasehold interest" do not include the interest of a tenant
in a periodic tenancy of one month or less. (Regulation 18233.) Since you have provided
thatMayor Pro Tem Talaugon has a seven-year lease on the property, this is an economic
interest to him as well.
A) Sale of the Coffee Shop
If Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon were to sell his coffee shop, this would affect his
economic interests in the following ways:
1) Investment and Business Position: He would no longer have an
economic interest in the business entity and he would no longer be an officer/employee.
These economic interests would no longer exist for the mayor pro tem for the Act's
conflict of interest considerations. Once the business is sold, it no longer constitutes an
investment interest in the business entity.
2) Source of Income: However, the purchaser of the coffee shop would
be a source of income for 12 months after the sale (assuming that the coffee shop is sold
for $500 or more). (See Crabb Advice Letter, No. 4-00-0661' Doane Advice Letter, No.
A-85-11 1.) The term "income" includes proceeds from any sale. (Section S2030(a).)
And, any income received by the mayor pro tem will still be disqualifuing for 12 months.
This means that the mayor pro tem will have to look back at the 12 months prior to the
date any decision is to be made to determine whether the coffee shop remains a source of
income to him. This will be so if he has received $500 ormore from the coffee shop in
that time period.. Payments fiom a source are aggregated.over the 12 rnonths prior to I ., ,, , ,
decision to determine if the source is apotentially disquali&ing economic interest. Thus,
in a case where periodic payments are made, the total received should be recalculated as
earlier payments become more than 12 months old.5
3) Real Property: Real property (assuming the lease can be transferred
to the buyer), once sold or transferred, no longer constitutes an economic interest to the
mayor pro tem. Therefore, if the mayor pro tem were to reassign the lease, he would no
longer have a real property interest in it.
If, on the other hand, Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon were to give the coffee shop to
his adult daughter in a bona fide, irrevocable transaction, it would no longer be an
economic interest in a business entity for Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon. As stated above, a
public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or
5 In other words, if $300 payments are received in January and March 2003, the offrcial will have
received more than $500 from a source of income in the 12 months prior to a December 2003 decision.
However, if the decision were made in February 2004,the official would have only received $300 in the
pnor 12 months because only the March 2003 income would still be counted.


File No. I-03-098
, 
page No. 6
liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is known as the "personal
financial effects" rule (sectio n ST l03, regulation lB7 o3.s). An offi cial's,.immediate
family" means the official's spouse and dependent children (Section B2o2g)
Also, a public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 57103(a);regulation
18703.1(a).) An indirect investment means, among other thingS, ffiy investment owned
by the official's spouse and dependent children (Section 37103.) A child is considered
"dependent" for purposes of the Act if the child is under 18 years old, and can be claimed
as a dependent for income tax purposes. (Nebb Advice Letter,No. I-00-017 ovrom
Advice Letter, No. 4-02-254.)
l) Investment and Business Position: Once an investrnent interest in a
business entity is disposed of by a bona fide, irrevocable transaction, it no longer creates
a conflict of interest for the public official disposing of the interest. In the Brazelton
Advice Letter, No. I-93-175, the public official disposed of an interest in a partnership
which was creating a conflict of interest by giving the interest to an adult child, whilJ
receiving no payment or other consideration for the transfer. (See also Doane Advice
Letter, No. 4-97-21 1.) In the Brazeltonletter, the adult child who received the transfer
was more than eighteen years old and was not a dependent of the public official. Thus,
the adult child was not a member of the public official's "immediate family,,, for
purposes of the Act. (Section82029.) This factor is important. If the recipient of the gift
had been a member of the public official's immediate family, then the gifted interest in
the business entity could still have posed a conflict of interest for the public official. (See
Section 87103 and regulation 18702.1(a)(a).) Also, the public official received no
payment from the adult child as consideration for the gift. Please note that the Act,s
.definition of "payment'2 is quite,broad, and includes'a transfer of anything of value,
tangible or intangible. (Section 82044.)
By making a bona fide, irrevocable gift of his coffee shop to an adult (i.e.,
eighteen years of age or older) child who is not his dependent, Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon
would no longer have an economic interest in the business entity because of investment
or business positions, for decisions on the development funds, the revitalizationof the
downtown area or questions regarding the chamber of commerce. We stress that the gift
must be bona fide and irrevocable. Any understanding, explicit or implicit, that the adult
child will later return the coffee shop to the mayor pro tem, or will provide to the mayor
pro tem any tangible or intangible benefit in return, would negate this advice.
2) Source of Income: However, any income received by the mayor pro
tem from the coffee shop will still be disqualiffing for 12 months. This means that the
mayor pro tem will have to look back at the 12 months prior to the date any decision is to
be made to determine whether the coffee shop remains a source of income to him. This
will be so if he has received $500 or more from the coffee shop in that time period.
Payments from a source are aggregated over the 12 months prior to a decision to
determine if the source is a potentially disqualifiiing economic interest. Thus, in a case


File No. I-03-098
Page No. 7
where periodic payments are made, the total received should be recalculated as earlier
payments become more than 12 months old.
3) Real Property: Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon's interest in the seven-year
lease will also be extinguished if it is transferred to his adult daughter. This means that
he will no longer have a disqualifying real property interest in the lease.
Step 4. WilI these economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
The fourth step in analyzing a potential conflict of interest is to determine whether
the official's economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decision at issue. (Regulation 18700(b)(a).) This step is important because it helps
determine which test for materiality to use in the step five, when deciding whethei it is
reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision will have amaterial financial
effect on the economic interests.
A business entity, a source of income, or a source of gifts is directly involved in a
decision when that person:
o 'olnitiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request [regulation 18704.1(a)(l)] or;
o "Is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding concerning the decision
before the official or the official's agency." A source of income is the "subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,denial orrevocation
of any license; permit,'or other'entitlement to, or contract with, the subject personl, or , ,
business entity. (Regulation I 8704. I ( a)(2).)
A) The coffee shop (all three decisions): Therefore, if Mayor pro Tem
Talaugon's coffee shop meets either standard listed above, it will be considered to be
directly involved in the decision. In addition, under the Commission's regulations, an
economic interest which is not directly involved under the rules stated above is
considered indirectly involved for purposes of choosing a materiality standard.
(Regulation 1 8704. I (b).) In this case, the coffee shop is not a named party in any of the
decisions. In fact, the decisions are more general, such as the revirtaliiation of the
downtown area,the use of redevelopment funds and the contemplation of the grand jury
report, which do not appeff to directly involve Mayor Pro Tern Talaugon's coffe" shop.u
B) Reat Property: When a public official has an economic interest in real
property, it is directly involved in a governmental decision if it "is the subject of the
govemmental decision," or if "any part of that real property, is located within 500 feet...
of the govemmental decision." (Regulat ion 187 04.2(a).)
6 The 500-foot rule for disqualification is not applicable to business entities, but is the rule for
determining if real property is directly involved in a decision as discussed below. (Regulation 18704.2.)


File No. I-03-098
Page No. 8
1) Grand Jury Investigation: As to the grand jury investigation, you
have indicated that Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon's property is less than 500 feet from the
chamber of commerce. The chamber of commerce may be included in the report. If it is,
then the city council will then have to decide what action to take on the report
recommendations. The chamber of commerce mentioned in the report could effect the
mayor pro tem's real property interest (the leasehold) if the chamber property or location
is the subject of the decision. You have provided no facts regarding this, therefore, we
can only set out the relevant standard for the public official to apply. If the chamber
property or location is the subject of the decision, then, since the coffee shop is located
within 500 feet of the property which would be the subject ofthe decision (the chamber
site), it would be directly involved in the decision. (Regulation fi7A4.2(a)(1).) If the
report is not about the chamber's property, then the mayor pro tem's real property interest
is indirectly involved.
When a public official's leasehold is indirectly involved in a decision, it is
presumednottobematerial. (Regulations 18704.2and,18705.2.) Sincewecannot
determine this without knowing the content of the grand jury report and how it affects the
chamber of commerce site, we can not further analyze this decision. Once the decision is
known, if the mayor pro tem cannot determine whether he has a conflict of interest based
on the information provided, then he can write in for additional advice on this question.
2) Revitalization of the Downtown Area: You have indicated that
Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon's property is less than 500 feet from the downtown area.
Therefore, his property is directly involved in the revitalization decisions using the 500-
foot distance test. (Regulation lS7 0a.2@)(l).)
:i 
3)'.Usaof,the Redevelopment Funds: You have indicated that Ma,yor,Pro , .
Tem Talaugon's property is less than 500 feet from the downtown area where the
redevelopment funds will be used. Therefore, his property is directly involved in the
decisions regarding the use of the redevelopment funds using the 500-foot distance test.
(Regulation t 87 0a.2@)(l).)
Step 5. Which materialitv standards should be used to decide if there will be a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect? 
.
A) The Coffee Shop (Revitalization of the Downtown Area & Use of the
Redevelopment Funds): If the coffee shop (economic interest) is not directly involved
in the governmental decision, regulation 18705.1(c) would be applicable. Under these
circumstances, since Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon's coffee shop is not listed on the Fortune
500, on the New York Stock Exchange, or the NASDAe/AMEX, subdivision (c)(4)
probably applies. Secondly, he must determine the increase or decrease in the dollar
amount the governmental decision would have on the business entity's gross revenues for
a fiscal year. After making these determinations, apply regulation 18705.1(c)(4). For
example, for a business entity that is not listed on the Fortune 500 or any exchange listed


File No. I-03-098
Page No. 9
above,T the regulation states that the decision will have a material financial effect on your
economic interest if
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a
fiscal year inthe amount of $20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The govemmental decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing
or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or
liabilities of $20,000 or more." (Regulation
I 870s. 1 (c)(4XA)-(c).)
A) Real Property (Revitalization of the Downtown Area & Use of the
Redevelopment Funds): However, for directly related real property, such as the seven-
year lease, regulation 18705.2(a) is applicable. In this case, the financial effect of the
governmental decision on the real property (leasehold) is presumed to be material.
Regulation 18705.2(a)(2) lists the proof which will rebut this presumption.
"This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not
reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision will
have any effect on any of the following:
r :,,r.:.: ,t.i . 
.i I ,
"(A) The termination date of the lease;
"(B) The amount of rent paid by the lessee for the
leased real property, either positively or negatively;
"(C) The value of the lessee's right to sublease the real
property, either positively or negatively;
"(D) The legally allowable use or the current use of the
real property by the lessee; or
7 Regulation 18705.1 provides different materiality ttuesholds depending on the financial size of a
business entity. Prior to January 16,2003, the middle two rankings could be applied where the business
was listed on the specified exchange or, in the alternative, if the business met the financial criteria for
listing on the exchange. (See subdivisions (c)(2) and (c)(3).) In January 2003,new altemate criteria for the
middle two rankings became effective. The new altemate criteria consider whether the business entities
meet specified thresholds for earnings before taxes or net income. Under the revised regulation, public
officials can apply the standards in subdivisions (c)(2) and (c)(3) if the business is listed on the specified
exchange, or if the business meets the specified earnings or income thresholds set forth in the regulation.


File No. I-03-098
Page No. l0
"(E) The use or enjoyment of the leased real property by
the lessee."
If this presumption is not rebutted, then the mateiality standard for this economic interest
has been met.
Step 6. Is it reasonabl!, foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect?
The sixth, and usually most important step, in deciding whether an official has a
conflict of interest is using the materiality standards (from step 5, above) to decide if a
material financial effect on one or more of your economic interests is reasonably
foreseeable as a result of the decision. (Regulation 18706.)
As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means "substantially likely." (Regulation
18706(a); In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial consequences
of a govemmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made is
highly situation-specific. A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered
reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to meet the
standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable. (Ibid.)
The Coffee Shop & Real Property (Revitalization of the Downtown Area &
Use of the Redevelopment Funds): You have provided no facts relevant to this part of
the analysis. If it is not reasonably foreseeable that the coffee shop would be materially
affected by the decisions, then Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon does not have a conflict of
interest and may participate. This is.a faotual determination that must b,e made by Mayor
Pro Tem Talaugon. However, the purpose of any redevelopment plan is to promote
sound development and redevelopment of blighted areas. The anticipated result of
redevelopment is an increase in property values within the project arca. (Howe Advice
Letter, No. A-00-148.) The very nature of redevelopment projects has led the
Commission to find that it is reasonably foreseeable that there will be some financial
effect on real property values and business interests located within or near project areas.
(In re Oglesby, supra; Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Com. (1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 983.) Therefore, the effect is reasonably foreseeable, and if the
materiality threshold is met, then the mayor pro tem has a conflict of interest in the
decisions relating to the redevelopment project
Steps 7. & 8. "Public Generally" and "Legally Required Participation,, Exceptions
These two exceptions should be applied if a conflict of interest is determined to
exist. Step seven is an exception that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and
mateial financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. (Section 87103, regulation 18707(a).) Under this
exception, if a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction is affected by the govemmental


File No. I-03-098
Page No. I1
decision in substantially the same manner as it would affect the public official, then the
official may participate in the decision.
You have asked specifically about special rules for small jurisdictions. please
note that regulation 18707.3, a special "public generally" rule applicable to small
jurisdictions was recently repealed by the Commission. Former regulation 18707.3, a
"public generally" exception that previously applied in small jurisdictions was repealed
effective February 25,2003. The only exception for small jurisdictions when applying
the conflict-of-interest rules is for retail business entities engaged in the sAe of gooas or
services. (Section 87103.5, copy enclosed.)
Step eight is an exception (regulation 18708) that applies when the official is
legally required to participate in the decision. Tl.pically, this exception is invoked when
a quorum cannot be constituted without the participation of the disqualified official.
There is nothing in the facts you supply to suggest that either of these exceptions applies
to Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon's involvement in these decisions. We have enclosed for
your information copies of regulations I 8707, I 87 07 .1, and I 87 07 .2, which address the
"public generally" exception, and regulation 18708, which addresses the "legally required
participation" exception.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter,please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General,Counsel
By:
Galena West
Couns el, Legal Division
Enclosures
Gwie

sections.question:
1. Does Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon have a conflict of interest in the city council,s
review of the grand jury report thatmay discuss the chamber of commerce when his
coffee shop is within 500 feet of the chamber?
2. Does Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon have a conflict of interest in the city council,s
discussion and vote regarding the downtown revitali zation since he owns acoffee shop
downtown?
2 Informal assistance does not pro-vide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 14; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.j in addition, this leuer
should not be construed as assistance on any conduct that may have already taken place.
3. Does Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon have a conflict of interest in the city council's
discussion and vote regarding the use of redevelopment funds since he owns a coffee
shop downtown?
4. Would Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon be disqualified for 12 months if he sold his
coffee shop?
5. If Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon gives his coffee shop to an adult child, would he then
immediately be able to participate in decisions regarding the downtown area from which
he is currently disqualified because of the coffee shop?
sections.conclusion:
l. Mayor Pro Tern Talaugon would have a conflict of interest in the city council's
review of the grand jury report that may discuss the chamber of commerce only if it is
reasonably foreseeable that his coffee shop will be materially financially effected as a
result of that discussion. We do not have enough facts to make this determination.
2. Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon would have a conflict of interest in the city council's
discussion and vote regarding the downtown revitalizationif it is reasonably foreseeable
that his coffee shop will be materially financially effected. I{, for instance, the downtown
revitalization project helps or hinders the downtown businesses, then a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect may be present for the mayor pro tem's coffee shop.
3. Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon would have a conflict of interest in the city council's
discussion and vote regarding the use of redevelopment funds if it is reasonably
' . foreseeable that his coffee shop will be materially financially effected. If for.instance,
the use of redevelopment funds helps or hinders the downtown businesses, then a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect may be present for the mayor pro tern's
coffee shop.
4. Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon would have an economic interest in the purchaser of his
coffee shop for 12 months after he sold his coffee shop.
5. By making a bona fide, irrevocable gift of his coffee shop to an adult child,
Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon still have an economic interest in the coffee shop for 12
months since the income he has received from the coffee shop is disquali$ingfor 12
months after reaching the $500 threshold for sources of income. Consequently, he will
have a conflict of interest in the decisions regarding the development of the downtown
area if there is a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the coffee shop since it wilt
remain an economic interest of his for 12 months.
sections.facts:
You represent Joe Talaugon, the mayorpro tem of the City of Guadalupe, a small
city of 5,800 people on the central coast of California. The mayor pro tem owns a small
business in downtown Guadalupe. It is a coffeehouse and ownership does not include the
building where the business is harbored. The revitalization of the downtown and
decisions about the use of redevelopment funds and related issues may affect businesses
or entities within 500 feet of his business.
During telephone conversations on May 23,2003 and June 17, 2003,you asked
an additional question on behalf of the mayor pro tem. You queried whether the mayor
pro tem could participate in the city council's discussions regarding a grand jury report
even though his coffee shop is located within 500 feet of the chamber of commerce,
which may be mentioned in the report. You also provided that the mayorpro tem has a
seven-year lease on the real property on which his business resides. This leases
colnmenced in 1998 and ends in 2005 and is renewable.
sections.analysis:
section 87100 prohibits any public official from "making," "participating in
making," or otherwise using his or her official position to "influence" a govemmental
decision in which the public official has a financial interest. The Commission has
adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an official has a
disqualifring conflict of interest (regulation 18700, subdivisions (b)(1) - (8), which is
discussed below. The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest may occur
whenever a public official makes a govemmental decision which mayhave a reasonably
foreseeable and material financial effect on one or more of his or her economic interests.
Steps 1. & 2. fs Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon a public official ttmaking.,,.oparticipating
in making." or 66influencing" a governmental decision?
i;.1. 
\ 
;,- ,,....-._. 
-.. :r.
The conflict-of-interest prohibition applies only to public officials. "Public
official" is defined as "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency. . . ." (Section 82048.) Since Mayor Pro Tern Talaugon is the mayor
pro tem of the City of Guadalupe, he is a public official who will be making, participating
in or influencing govemmental decisions within the meaning of the Act. (Section ST1OO;
regulations 787 01, 187 02.1 - 187 02.3.)
Step 3. What are Mayor Pro Tem Talaugonrs economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from
economic interests. The economic interests from which conflicts of interest may arise are
defined in regulations 18703-18703.5. Identifting which, if any, of these economic
interests are held by a public official is the third step in analyzing a potential conflict of
interest under the Act. (Regulation 18700(b)(3).) There are six possible economic
interests:
1. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation
I 8703.1(a));


PageNo.4
2. A public official has an economic interest in which he or she is a director, fficer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d);
regulation I 8703. 1 (b));
3. A public official has an economic interest in real property inwhich he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation lS7O3.Z);
4. A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 I 03(c); regulation 187 03.3)3 ;
5. A public official has an economic interest in any source of Stfts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87 I 03(e); regulation 187 $.9;
6. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is known as
the "personal financial effects" rule (section 87103, regulation r s703.5).
The information provided only refers to Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon's coffee shop,
so this analysis will be limited to the economic interests that derive from it. Mayor pro
Tem Talaugon is the sole owner of his coffee shop.
The Coffee Shop: As sole owner of the coffee shop, the mayor pro tem has an
economic interest in this business entity as an officer/employee of the company as well as
an investor. (Sections 87103(a) and (d); regulation 18703.1(a) and O).) Additionally, he
has an economic interest in the shop because of the income he has received frorn it. , ., ,
(Section 87103(c).)
Customers: Pursuant to sections 87103(c) and 82030, Mayor Pro Tern Talaugon
would also have an interest in an individual who is a source of income to his shop sinie
his ownership interest in the coffee shop is 100%. He will have an economic interest in a
particular client, such as the chamber oico-m..ce, if the income attributed to him from
the client will aggregate to $500 or more during the relevant 12 month time period. Since
you have not provided facts relating to customers, we will forego any remaining analysis
of this economic interest." As to the chamber of commerce, if the association itself has
not been a client of his coffee shop, the chamber is not an economic interest of his.
Purchases of coffee from individual members are not aggregated. In other words, the
members'purchases do not count toward the $500. If the answer is no, then the chamber
is not an economic interest ofhis and the chamber's proximity to the coffee shop is
relevant to our conflicts analysis only if it is reasonably foreseeable that the coffee shop
itself is materially affected as discussed below under step four.
a You should request additional advice concerning the customers of the coffee shop if you believe
that they may be materially impacted by the decision.
ReaI Property: Under the Act, an "interest in real property" includes a leasehold
interest in real property within the official's jurisdiction. (Section82033.) The terms
'tnterest in real property" and "leasehold interest" do not include the interest of a tenant
in a periodic tenancy of one month or less. (Regulation 18233.) Since you have provided
thatMayor Pro Tem Talaugon has a seven-year lease on the property, this is an economic
interest to him as well.
A) Sale of the Coffee Shop
If Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon were to sell his coffee shop, this would affect his
economic interests in the following ways:
1) Investment and Business Position: He would no longer have an
economic interest in the business entity and he would no longer be an officer/employee.
These economic interests would no longer exist for the mayor pro tem for the Act's
conflict of interest considerations. Once the business is sold, it no longer constitutes an
investment interest in the business entity.
2) Source of Income: However, the purchaser of the coffee shop would
be a source of income for 12 months after the sale (assuming that the coffee shop is sold
for $500 or more). (See Crabb Advice Letter, No. 4-00-0661' Doane Advice Letter, No.
A-85-11 1.) The term "income" includes proceeds from any sale. (Section S2030(a).)
And, any income received by the mayor pro tem will still be disqualifuing for 12 months.
This means that the mayor pro tem will have to look back at the 12 months prior to the
date any decision is to be made to determine whether the coffee shop remains a source of
income to him. This will be so if he has received $500 ormore from the coffee shop in
that time period.. Payments fiom a source are aggregated.over the 12 rnonths prior to I ., ,, , ,
decision to determine if the source is apotentially disquali&ing economic interest. Thus,
in a case where periodic payments are made, the total received should be recalculated as
earlier payments become more than 12 months old.5
3) Real Property: Real property (assuming the lease can be transferred
to the buyer), once sold or transferred, no longer constitutes an economic interest to the
mayor pro tem. Therefore, if the mayor pro tem were to reassign the lease, he would no
longer have a real property interest in it.
If, on the other hand, Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon were to give the coffee shop to
his adult daughter in a bona fide, irrevocable transaction, it would no longer be an
economic interest in a business entity for Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon. As stated above, a
public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or
5 In other words, if $300 payments are received in January and March 2003, the offrcial will have
received more than $500 from a source of income in the 12 months prior to a December 2003 decision.
However, if the decision were made in February 2004,the official would have only received $300 in the
pnor 12 months because only the March 2003 income would still be counted.


, 
liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is known as the "personal
financial effects" rule (sectio n ST l03, regulation lB7 o3.s). An offi cial's,.immediate
family" means the official's spouse and dependent children (Section B2o2g)
Also, a public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 57103(a);regulation
18703.1(a).) An indirect investment means, among other thingS, ffiy investment owned
by the official's spouse and dependent children (Section 37103.) A child is considered
"dependent" for purposes of the Act if the child is under 18 years old, and can be claimed
as a dependent for income tax purposes. (Nebb Advice Letter,No. I-00-017 ovrom
Advice Letter, No. 4-02-254.)
l) Investment and Business Position: Once an investrnent interest in a
business entity is disposed of by a bona fide, irrevocable transaction, it no longer creates
a conflict of interest for the public official disposing of the interest. In the Brazelton
Advice Letter, No. I-93-175, the public official disposed of an interest in a partnership
which was creating a conflict of interest by giving the interest to an adult child, whilJ
receiving no payment or other consideration for the transfer. (See also Doane Advice
Letter, No. 4-97-21 1.) In the Brazeltonletter, the adult child who received the transfer
was more than eighteen years old and was not a dependent of the public official. Thus,
the adult child was not a member of the public official's "immediate family,,, for
purposes of the Act. (Section82029.) This factor is important. If the recipient of the gift
had been a member of the public official's immediate family, then the gifted interest in
the business entity could still have posed a conflict of interest for the public official. (See
Section 87103 and regulation 18702.1(a)(a).) Also, the public official received no
payment from the adult child as consideration for the gift. Please note that the Act,s
.definition of "payment'2 is quite,broad, and includes'a transfer of anything of value,
tangible or intangible. (Section 82044.)
By making a bona fide, irrevocable gift of his coffee shop to an adult (i.e.,
eighteen years of age or older) child who is not his dependent, Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon
would no longer have an economic interest in the business entity because of investment
or business positions, for decisions on the development funds, the revitalizationof the
downtown area or questions regarding the chamber of commerce. We stress that the gift
must be bona fide and irrevocable. Any understanding, explicit or implicit, that the adult
child will later return the coffee shop to the mayor pro tem, or will provide to the mayor
pro tem any tangible or intangible benefit in return, would negate this advice.
2) Source of Income: However, any income received by the mayor pro
tem from the coffee shop will still be disqualiffing for 12 months. This means that the
mayor pro tem will have to look back at the 12 months prior to the date any decision is to
be made to determine whether the coffee shop remains a source of income to him. This
will be so if he has received $500 or more from the coffee shop in that time period.
Payments from a source are aggregated over the 12 months prior to a decision to
determine if the source is a potentially disqualifiiing economic interest. Thus, in a case
where periodic payments are made, the total received should be recalculated as earlier
payments become more than 12 months old.
3) Real Property: Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon's interest in the seven-year
lease will also be extinguished if it is transferred to his adult daughter. This means that
he will no longer have a disqualifying real property interest in the lease.
Step 4. WilI these economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
The fourth step in analyzing a potential conflict of interest is to determine whether
the official's economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decision at issue. (Regulation 18700(b)(a).) This step is important because it helps
determine which test for materiality to use in the step five, when deciding whethei it is
reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision will have amaterial financial
effect on the economic interests.
A business entity, a source of income, or a source of gifts is directly involved in a
decision when that person:
o 'olnitiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request [regulation 18704.1(a)(l)] or;
o "Is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding concerning the decision
before the official or the official's agency." A source of income is the "subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,denial orrevocation
of any license; permit,'or other'entitlement to, or contract with, the subject personl, or , ,
business entity. (Regulation I 8704. I ( a)(2).)
A) The coffee shop (all three decisions): Therefore, if Mayor pro Tem
Talaugon's coffee shop meets either standard listed above, it will be considered to be
directly involved in the decision. In addition, under the Commission's regulations, an
economic interest which is not directly involved under the rules stated above is
considered indirectly involved for purposes of choosing a materiality standard.
(Regulation 1 8704. I (b).) In this case, the coffee shop is not a named party in any of the
decisions. In fact, the decisions are more general, such as the revirtaliiation of the
downtown area,the use of redevelopment funds and the contemplation of the grand jury
report, which do not appeff to directly involve Mayor Pro Tern Talaugon's coffe" shop.u
B) Reat Property: When a public official has an economic interest in real
property, it is directly involved in a governmental decision if it "is the subject of the
govemmental decision," or if "any part of that real property, is located within 500 feet...
of the govemmental decision." (Regulat ion 187 04.2(a).)
6 The 500-foot rule for disqualification is not applicable to business entities, but is the rule for
determining if real property is directly involved in a decision as discussed below. (Regulation 18704.2.)
1) Grand Jury Investigation: As to the grand jury investigation, you
have indicated that Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon's property is less than 500 feet from the
chamber of commerce. The chamber of commerce may be included in the report. If it is,
then the city council will then have to decide what action to take on the report
recommendations. The chamber of commerce mentioned in the report could effect the
mayor pro tem's real property interest (the leasehold) if the chamber property or location
is the subject of the decision. You have provided no facts regarding this, therefore, we
can only set out the relevant standard for the public official to apply. If the chamber
property or location is the subject of the decision, then, since the coffee shop is located
within 500 feet of the property which would be the subject ofthe decision (the chamber
site), it would be directly involved in the decision. (Regulation fi7A4.2(a)(1).) If the
report is not about the chamber's property, then the mayor pro tem's real property interest
is indirectly involved.
When a public official's leasehold is indirectly involved in a decision, it is
presumednottobematerial. (Regulations 18704.2and,18705.2.) Sincewecannot
determine this without knowing the content of the grand jury report and how it affects the
chamber of commerce site, we can not further analyze this decision. Once the decision is
known, if the mayor pro tem cannot determine whether he has a conflict of interest based
on the information provided, then he can write in for additional advice on this question.
2) Revitalization of the Downtown Area: You have indicated that
Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon's property is less than 500 feet from the downtown area.
Therefore, his property is directly involved in the revitalization decisions using the 500-
foot distance test. (Regulation lS7 0a.2@)(l).)
:i 
3)'.Usaof,the Redevelopment Funds: You have indicated that Ma,yor,Pro , .
Tem Talaugon's property is less than 500 feet from the downtown area where the
redevelopment funds will be used. Therefore, his property is directly involved in the
decisions regarding the use of the redevelopment funds using the 500-foot distance test.
(Regulation t 87 0a.2@)(l).)
Step 5. Which materialitv standards should be used to decide if there will be a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect? 
.
A) The Coffee Shop (Revitalization of the Downtown Area & Use of the
Redevelopment Funds): If the coffee shop (economic interest) is not directly involved
in the governmental decision, regulation 18705.1(c) would be applicable. Under these
circumstances, since Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon's coffee shop is not listed on the Fortune
500, on the New York Stock Exchange, or the NASDAe/AMEX, subdivision (c)(4)
probably applies. Secondly, he must determine the increase or decrease in the dollar
amount the governmental decision would have on the business entity's gross revenues for
a fiscal year. After making these determinations, apply regulation 18705.1(c)(4). For
example, for a business entity that is not listed on the Fortune 500 or any exchange listed
above,T the regulation states that the decision will have a material financial effect on your
economic interest if
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a
fiscal year inthe amount of $20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The govemmental decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing
or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or
liabilities of $20,000 or more." (Regulation
I 870s. 1 (c)(4XA)-(c).)
A) Real Property (Revitalization of the Downtown Area & Use of the
Redevelopment Funds): However, for directly related real property, such as the seven-
year lease, regulation 18705.2(a) is applicable. In this case, the financial effect of the
governmental decision on the real property (leasehold) is presumed to be material.
Regulation 18705.2(a)(2) lists the proof which will rebut this presumption.
"This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not
reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision will
have any effect on any of the following:
r :,,r.:.: ,t.i . 
.i I ,
"(A) The termination date of the lease;
"(B) The amount of rent paid by the lessee for the
leased real property, either positively or negatively;
"(C) The value of the lessee's right to sublease the real
property, either positively or negatively;
"(D) The legally allowable use or the current use of the
real property by the lessee; or
7 Regulation 18705.1 provides different materiality ttuesholds depending on the financial size of a
business entity. Prior to January 16,2003, the middle two rankings could be applied where the business
was listed on the specified exchange or, in the alternative, if the business met the financial criteria for
listing on the exchange. (See subdivisions (c)(2) and (c)(3).) In January 2003,new altemate criteria for the
middle two rankings became effective. The new altemate criteria consider whether the business entities
meet specified thresholds for earnings before taxes or net income. Under the revised regulation, public
officials can apply the standards in subdivisions (c)(2) and (c)(3) if the business is listed on the specified
exchange, or if the business meets the specified earnings or income thresholds set forth in the regulation.


Page No. l0
"(E) The use or enjoyment of the leased real property by
the lessee."
If this presumption is not rebutted, then the mateiality standard for this economic interest
has been met.
Step 6. Is it reasonabl!, foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect?
The sixth, and usually most important step, in deciding whether an official has a
conflict of interest is using the materiality standards (from step 5, above) to decide if a
material financial effect on one or more of your economic interests is reasonably
foreseeable as a result of the decision. (Regulation 18706.)
As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means "substantially likely." (Regulation
18706(a); In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial consequences
of a govemmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made is
highly situation-specific. A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered
reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to meet the
standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable. (Ibid.)
The Coffee Shop & Real Property (Revitalization of the Downtown Area &
Use of the Redevelopment Funds): You have provided no facts relevant to this part of
the analysis. If it is not reasonably foreseeable that the coffee shop would be materially
affected by the decisions, then Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon does not have a conflict of
interest and may participate. This is.a faotual determination that must b,e made by Mayor
Pro Tem Talaugon. However, the purpose of any redevelopment plan is to promote
sound development and redevelopment of blighted areas. The anticipated result of
redevelopment is an increase in property values within the project arca. (Howe Advice
Letter, No. A-00-148.) The very nature of redevelopment projects has led the
Commission to find that it is reasonably foreseeable that there will be some financial
effect on real property values and business interests located within or near project areas.
(In re Oglesby, supra; Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Com. (1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 983.) Therefore, the effect is reasonably foreseeable, and if the
materiality threshold is met, then the mayor pro tem has a conflict of interest in the
decisions relating to the redevelopment project
Steps 7. & 8. "Public Generally" and "Legally Required Participation,, Exceptions
These two exceptions should be applied if a conflict of interest is determined to
exist. Step seven is an exception that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and
mateial financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. (Section 87103, regulation 18707(a).) Under this
exception, if a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction is affected by the govemmental


Page No. I1
decision in substantially the same manner as it would affect the public official, then the
official may participate in the decision.
You have asked specifically about special rules for small jurisdictions. please
note that regulation 18707.3, a special "public generally" rule applicable to small
jurisdictions was recently repealed by the Commission. Former regulation 18707.3, a
"public generally" exception that previously applied in small jurisdictions was repealed
effective February 25,2003. The only exception for small jurisdictions when applying
the conflict-of-interest rules is for retail business entities engaged in the sAe of gooas or
services. (Section 87103.5, copy enclosed.)
Step eight is an exception (regulation 18708) that applies when the official is
legally required to participate in the decision. Tl.pically, this exception is invoked when
a quorum cannot be constituted without the participation of the disqualified official.
There is nothing in the facts you supply to suggest that either of these exceptions applies
to Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon's involvement in these decisions. We have enclosed for
your information copies of regulations I 8707, I 87 07 .1, and I 87 07 .2, which address the
"public generally" exception, and regulation 18708, which addresses the "legally required
participation" exception.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon have a conflict of interest in the city council,s
review of the grand jury report thatmay discuss the chamber of commerce when his
coffee shop is within 500 feet of the chamber?
2. Does Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon have a conflict of interest in the city council,s
discussion and vote regarding the downtown revitali zation since he owns acoffee shop
downtown?
2 Informal assistance does not pro-vide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 14; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.j in addition, this leuer
should not be construed as assistance on any conduct that may have already taken place.
3. Does Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon have a conflict of interest in the city council's
discussion and vote regarding the use of redevelopment funds since he owns a coffee
shop downtown?
4. Would Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon be disqualified for 12 months if he sold his
coffee shop?
5. If Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon gives his coffee shop to an adult child, would he then
immediately be able to participate in decisions regarding the downtown area from which
he is currently disqualified because of the coffee shop?

CONCLUSION: l. Mayor Pro Tern Talaugon would have a conflict of interest in the city council's
review of the grand jury report that may discuss the chamber of commerce only if it is
reasonably foreseeable that his coffee shop will be materially financially effected as a
result of that discussion. We do not have enough facts to make this determination.
2. Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon would have a conflict of interest in the city council's
discussion and vote regarding the downtown revitalizationif it is reasonably foreseeable
that his coffee shop will be materially financially effected. I{, for instance, the downtown
revitalization project helps or hinders the downtown businesses, then a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect may be present for the mayor pro tem's coffee shop.
3. Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon would have a conflict of interest in the city council's
discussion and vote regarding the use of redevelopment funds if it is reasonably
' . foreseeable that his coffee shop will be materially financially effected. If for.instance,
the use of redevelopment funds helps or hinders the downtown businesses, then a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect may be present for the mayor pro tern's
coffee shop.
4. Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon would have an economic interest in the purchaser of his
coffee shop for 12 months after he sold his coffee shop.
5. By making a bona fide, irrevocable gift of his coffee shop to an adult child,
Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon still have an economic interest in the coffee shop for 12
months since the income he has received from the coffee shop is disquali$ingfor 12
months after reaching the $500 threshold for sources of income. Consequently, he will
have a conflict of interest in the decisions regarding the development of the downtown
area if there is a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the coffee shop since it wilt
remain an economic interest of his for 12 months.

Mayor Pro Tem Talaugon would have a conflict of interest in the city
